text: Good morning. I want to thank SAGES for giving me the opportunity to present here. My name is Brandon Andrew. I'm an MIS fellow at the University of Pittsburgh Medical Center. Today I'll be presenting super mesenteric artery syndrome following gastric bypass.

We present a case of a thirty-three-year-old female, twelve weeks pregnant at presentation, who presented with two days of epigastric abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. Her surgical history is significant for gastric bypass in two thousand fourteen. She did require a return to the operating room for over somewhere GJ staple line bleed shortly after her procedure. She had significant weight loss BMI declined from fifty to twenty six which is a ninety two pound weight loss for her. On exam she presented with upper abdominal distention and mild tenderness. An MRI was obtained and that showed severely distended gastric remnant and duodenum.

During the diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon observes an incredibly dilated gastric remnant. Adhesions involving the BP limb are identified and divided using energy, including a thick adhesion at the proximal portion. The third portion of the duodenum shows some improvement. Adhesions involving the rullum are also divided. A G-tube is then placed into the gastric remnant.

The patient tolerated PO intake for several days. However, she began to have persistently elevated G-tube output and ultimately recurrence of nausea and vomiting with PO intake. At this point, we obtained a contrast study through the G-tube in the gastrogrammatin seen here, which showed limited outflow through the duodenum. We then obtained the CT, which showed narrowing between the aorta and SMA vessels. The duodenal diameter at this location was measured as five millimeters. These findings suggested a diagnosis of superior mesenteric artery syndrome.

The surgical team prepares for diagnostic laparoscopy and gastroduodenoscopy via the remnant G-tube. Ports are placed in their previous positions, and adhesions are lysed. The three limbs of the gastric bypass are visible. The third portion of the duodenum appears distended. The transverse mesocolon is divided to expose the duodenum, revealing the underlying vena cava.

The surgical team completes the dissection to expose the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) vessels. A pediatric endoscope is advanced through the gastrostomy into the duodenum, navigating through a narrowed area at the third portion of the duodenum where pulsations from the SMA are visible. The team confirms the diagnosis of SMA syndrome and proceeds with a duodenal jejunal anastomosis using the common channel jejunum, placing an anchoring suture.

The surgeon creates enterotomies and performs a stapled anastomosis, approximating the common enterotomy with interrupted sutures. A stapled closure is completed, and an anti-obstruction stitch is placed. A drain is left in the area of the anastomosis. Postoperative imaging shows no dilatation of the gastric remnant and contrast flow through the anastomosis.

Superior mesenteric artery syndrome is seen in patients with dramatic weight loss as can be seen after a gastric bypass. These patients can lose one hundred percent or more of their excess body weight. These patients present with nonspecific symptoms of postprandial nausea and epigastric pain. CT can demonstrate an aorta mesenteric angle less than fifteen degrees or duodenal diameter at the estimate of less than eight millimeters. Surgical options can include division of the ligament of trites or more definitive surgical bypass such as duodenal jejunostomy. Thirteen cases of SMA syndrome have been reported in literature after gastric bypass. These patients typically undergo an extensive workup with non-diagnostic radiographic imaging and often operative exploration for other diagnoses prior to making the diagnosis of SMA syndrome. And so surgeons should have a high index of suspicion to diagnosis of rare complication in patients who have substantial weight loss after gastric bypass surgery and present with recurrent intermittent obstructive symptoms that cannot clearly be attributed to another source.
